title: The Macro Minute: Is US Treasury still boosting Paradigm C? Is our Resilient US Economy theme valid?

Happy Wednesday out there team42 at your
skipper here at Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Wednesday July 30th
2025. Hope everyone's having a great
week. So as always we'll start with our
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you haven't been
following our KISS or Dr. Mo signals
need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro questions are, is the
US Treasury still contributing to our
bullish paradigm seat theme? And is our
three-year-old resilient US economy
theme still valid? Our answers are hard
yes in terms of the Treasury and soft
yes in terms of uh our resilient US
economy theme. Regarding the Treasury,
part two of the Q3 quarterly refunding
statement, which features the debt
issuance composition, was consistent
with our guidance for Secretary Besson
and his team to avoid interrupting our
bullish paradigm. MC theme which we
authored back in April. The most
positive news from the Q3 QR was the
fact that the Treasury maintained its
coupon auction sizes and preserved its
guidance to leave them unchanged for the
foreseeable future. Also very positive
was the Treasury's decision to replenish
the dep Treasury general account balance
with bills instead of coupons. As
discussed in our July 19th around the
horn presentation, this is a clear
signal that the administration does not
want to do anything that upsets the
apple cart from the perspective of
engineering a boom in the economy and
asset markets. Regarding the resiliency
of the US economy, the Q2 GDP report,
June Jolts report, and July Conference
Board Consumer Confidence Report each
supported our U-shaped economy thesis, a
pillar of support for our resilient US
economy theme. The June Jones report and
July conference board consumer
confidence report both contributed to
our labor hoarding thesis. Another
pillar of support for our resilient US
economy theme. The prepoundonderance of
recent evidence gives us confidence to
reiterate our call for US economic and
earnings growth to generally and
persistently surprise consents
expectations to the upside over the
medium term. Uh so that's in our opinion
even though growth is slowing the fact
that it is generally and persistently
surprising to the upside as we've been
calling for since we authored the
paradigm C theme is part of the reason
you know we made new highs and in in
risk assets like stocks and bitcoin etc.
Um, so transitioning to our 42 micro
dashboard as always wrap up with a
question from our community. Uh, this
one's uh titled wartime economy. Uh,
says Darius always talks about seeing
the forest through the trees. I believe
the force is that we are in an AI
race/cold war with China. And it seems
like the administration understands this
and is admitting it publicly. With that
said, it seems that to win this
race/war, the Fed is going to have to
shift from inflation/job mandates to
supporting the fiscal spending that
needs to happen ASAP to catch up with
China. The amount of buildout that needs
to happen ASAP is insane. I'm actually a
bit shocked that the administration
isn't putting even more pressure on the
need to win this race. It seems like the
printer needs to come on ASAP simply for
the sake of national security. It seems
like a wartime economy and and Fed is in
the near future. uh sorry wartime
economy and Fed uh is in the near future
and probably not priced in. Uh in our
view it's not priced in for for the
record. Uh everyone is talking about
tariffs and thinking the Fed shouldn't
cut. I haven't heard many economists
talking about the AI race/war that is
going to require a shift in the Fed's
mandate. It seems like the Fed needs to
shift to support this war and most don't
understand that we are in. It seems
silly to worry about inflation when we
need power plants and data centers built
yesterday.
Fantastic question.
This is exactly what we've been
preparing our clients for for two years
now in the context of our investing
during a forward turning regime
framework. Uh recall that in the summer
of 2023 uh we did a deep big deep dive
study on the for turning I'm pulling up
our forturning FAQ here. Uh and you know
the the the one of the key takeaways of

clients you know maintain the confidence

from the presentation particularly uh as
it relates to uh um you know sort of the
Feds being forced incrementally and
persistently into supporting uh uh the
fiscal dominance regime. And so, uh,

tend to see in four attorneys, which is
fiscal dominance, geopolitical tension,
breakdown of global trade, um, and
ultimately the Fed having to support,
you know, fiscal dominance as the threat
of war uh, rises and eventually, you
know, becomes 100% probability uh, uh,
uh, a certainty rather. Uh so um you
know so I agree with a lot of the the
conclusions of the the conversation
which is the the Fed being forced
incrementally into fiscal dominance i.e.
what we've been talking about over the
past few weeks which is or we we've sort
of renamed it uh the Treasury will elbow
the Fed at some point in this fourth
turning if you know and so uh we
continue to see that as a high
probability upside risk for risk assets
and gold and high probability downside
risk for the US dollar over the long
term. again, doesn't have to happen
anytime soon, but in our view, that's
where we're headed. And so as an
investor, you have to assume that uh if
your portfolio is long a bunch of
nominal bonds uh as opposed to long
things like stocks going to Bitcoin,
which is how we have our client's
position in our KISS model portfolio, uh
you're going to struggle in in in this
kind of regime as we move forward in
time because again we are in our wartime
economy will become which and that
outcome will become increasingly uh uh
it will become increasingly uh obvious
as we move forward in time. Uh so uh
like I said, kudos to the client for
framing it that way. I think that's an
appropriate way to frame it. Um you
know, now we'll talk about the societal
implications about AI and all these
other things in another day, but I just
think the the the real key takeaway here
is that markets are not priced for
significant regime change at the Federal
Reserve. We've been talking about it and
they're moving in that direction, but
they're not adequately priced for a gene
change at the Federal Reserve. And so
again, as I mentioned, that is right
tail risk for risk assets in gold, left
tail risk for treasury bonds and the US
dollar. But ultimately, the Fed can use
its balance sheet to cap yields in the
bond market in so much that as we talked
about earlier in the executive summary,
the Treasury can continue, you know,
persisting with dovish net financing
policy to also uh cap yields on the long
end of the bond market. And so uh these
are all things financial pressure,
accelerated monetary debasement, you
know, fiscal support or monetary policy
support for fiscal dominance. These are
all things that have historically
happened in four attorneys as as we
determined in our empirical deep dive
study on this subject matter and these
are things you need to expect as an
investor moving forward. So if you want
